body.rootCause: Temporary mechanical/electrical malfunction of the freezer unit.
body.detailsOfNC: The freezer was found not maintaining the required storage temperature due to equipment malfunction.
body.correctiveAction:
The issue was reported to the maintenance department immediately upon detection.
Maintenance personnel attended the breakdown and completed the repair within 2 hours of identification.
The freezer was tested after repair and confirmed to be operating normally within the required temperature range.
Product temperatures were checked and verified to be within acceptable limits.
Temperature monitoring was continued to confirm stable operation.
body.preventiveAction:
Continue routine preventive maintenance of refrigeration equipment.
Reinforce daily temperature monitoring and verification.
Ensure prompt reporting and response to any equipment abnormalities.

body.rootCause:
5 Why Analysis
Why did the customer complain that the raw Tikka Extra was very tough with an unpleasant liquid consistency? Because the raw meat muscle fibers were overly dense/stringy, and the marinade had separated from the meat, creating a watery, slimy pooling effect in the packaging.
Why did the marinade separate and cause a liquid consistency? Because the raw meat experienced a severe "moisture purge," meaning the muscle cells completely failed to bind and retain the liquid marinade.
Why did the raw meat fail to bind the marinade and remain tough? Because a specific batch of raw meat possessed a high collagen content or a high pH variance which naturally resists tenderization and lacks water-holding capacity.
Why was this specific tough/non-binding raw meat used in production? Because during the raw meat preparation and dicing stage, an isolated piece of tough, low-quality primal cut slipped through without being sorted out by the line operators.
Why did the low-quality raw meat slip through preparation undetected? Because the preparation line relies on rapid manual dicing, and there was no strict raw material specification check or sorting step to flag and discard tough, stringy connective tissue before adding the marinade.
body.detailsOfNC: The item she has issue with is Tikka Extra, she said it was very tough and had an unpleasant liquid consistency. She mentioned the quality was not up to the usual standard.
body.correctiveAction:
Apologized to the customer, arranged a swift replacement of a fresh batch of raw Tikka Extra, and issued a credit note.
Immediately blocked and inspected all remaining raw Tikka Extra packs from the same production batch code in our chillers.
body.preventiveAction: Implemented a mandatory "liquid separation check" right before the raw marinated product is sealed into its final packaging to catch any excess liquid pooling early.

body.rootCause:
5 Why Analysis
Why did the customer receive a topside with an unpleasant color? The surface of the meat suffered oxidation and discoloration.
Why did oxidation occur on only 1-2 pieces? The anaerobic vacuum packaging failed on those specific pieces.
Why did the packaging fail? Micro-punctures or tears ("leakers") developed due to friction and abrasive rubbing between cartons during maritime transit.
Why was it missed during receiving inspection? The warehouse team followed standard batch-wise random sampling, which statistically can miss an isolated defect hidden inside a large pallet.
What is the Root Cause? The supplier's packaging layer lacked heavy-gauge puncture resistance or protective corner padding to withstand transit vibrations.
body.detailsOfNC: Received a complaint regarding the quality of the Brazilian Topside delivered on 08/04/2026 (see attached invoice),The customer noted an unpleasant color, which you can see in the attached video.
body.correctiveAction:
Picked up the defective meat and issued a premium replacement batch immediately.
Conducted a physical inspection on all remaining inventory from the same batch to guarantee no other leaking packages existed.
body.preventiveAction: Increased the receiving quality inspection sample size from 5% to 15% for imported primes. Inspectors must now perform a tactile check (manually pressing the pack) to check for soft vacuum seals.

body.rootCause:
5 Why Analysis
Why did the customer complain about a bad quality beef leg? Because the specific piece they received had localized spoilage/bad odor.
Why did this bad quality piece reach the customer? Because it was missed during our internal receiving inspection.
Why was it missed during the receiving inspection? Because our standard receiving protocol only requires a batch-wise random sample inspection, which naturally has a statistical chance of missing 1 or 2 bad pieces hidden in a large bulk quantity.
Why were there bad pieces in the bulk quantity in the first place? Because of an isolated operational variance at the supplier's facility in Pakistan (e.g., poor handling, delayed chilling, or improper trimming of that specific carcass).
Why did the supplier ship these bad quality pieces? (Root Cause) Because the supplier lacks a robust piece-by-piece final quality check before boxing/exporting the beef legs.
body.detailsOfNC: received a complaint from the customer for the bad quality of Pakistani beef leg, as he found one pc,
body.correctiveAction:
Replaced the bad quality beef leg for the customer immediately to maintain satisfaction.
Checked all remaining Pakistan beef leg inventory currently in our chillers to ensure no other bad pieces were present.
body.preventiveAction:
Supplier Notification: Sent a formal complaint to the Pakistan supplier with the batch/box details, demanding they investigate their packing line for that specific production date.

Placed incoming Pakistan beef leg shipments on "High Alert" for the next 3 deliveries, meaning inspectors will do a deeper check to ensure the supplier's corrective steps are working.

body.rootCause:
5 Why Analysis
Why did the customer report an unpleasant odor? Anaerobic bacteria began producing gases within the deep muscle or bone joints of specific carcasses.
Why was the odor not detected during the warehouse receiving inspection? The cold air circulation in the chiller and the low surface temperature (< 5) suppressed the odor molecules. The odor only became volatile (noticeable) when the customer moved the carcass to a warmer processing area or began cutting into the deep tissue.
Why did only 1 or 2 pieces have an odor while the rest were fine? Those specific carcasses likely had a higher initial microbial load or suffered from "Bone Taint"—where the heat wasn't pulled out of the heavy joints (hip/shoulder) fast enough at the slaughterhouse.
Why would "Bone Taint" occur if the shipment temperature was maintained? The issue occurred pre-shipment. If the carcass was large/fat or the supplier's "active chilling" phase was insufficient, the internal bone temperature stayed warm too long, allowing bacteria to grow before the carcass was ever loaded for export.
Why did the quality process fail to identify this before delivery? (Root Cause): The inspection protocol is limited to surface-level checks (external temp/visual). It does not include internal/deep-tissue sensory checks for heavy-muscled Australian carcasses which are prone to internal spoilage if the initial chill-down is suboptimal.
body.detailsOfNC: The customer reported dissatisfaction with the Australian lamb carcass he received today, he said it has unpleasant smell and color (pics attached)
body.correctiveAction:
Isolated and rejected the 1–2 smelly lamb carcasses immediately.
Inspected the rest of the batch; confirmed all other carcasses were at <4∘C with no odor, and cleared them for use.
Safely disposed of the rejected carcasses as waste.
Raised a formal complaint with the Australian supplier with the carcass tag numbers for their internal review.
body.preventiveAction:
Updated the receiving check to include a quick sensory (smell) test on a sample of carcasses for all future shipments, even if the delivery temperature is perfect.
Set a rule to perform extra quality checks on the next 3 deliveries from this supplier to verify consistency.

body.rootCause:
5 Why Analysis	Findings

Why did the meat have bad smell?	Product quality deteriorated.
Why did quality deteriorate?	Possible handling or temperature exposure during dispatch/return handling.
Why was handling issue not identified?	Dispatch inspection verification was insufficient.
Why was verification insufficient?	Staff did not fully follow verification procedure.
Why procedure was not fully followed?	Need for refresher training and stricter monitoring.
body.detailsOfNC: As per the customer, the Pakistani leg meat received had a very bad smell and unacceptable condition
body.correctiveAction:
Additional inspection conducted on all meat items.
Complaint communicated to relevant department.
Staff instructed to strengthen product checking before dispatch.
body.preventiveAction:
Daily meat inspection checklist reinforced.
Dispatch verification made mandatory before delivery.
Return product inspection procedure strengthened.
Refresher training conducted for meat handling staff.
QA verification increased for chilled meat products.

body.detailsOfNC: The Customer found a mosquito inside the packaging after opening the Pakistani beef product.
body.correctiveAction:
- Apologize to the customer and replace the product ASAP.
- Inform the supplier about the issue with evidence (photos, etc.).
- Request an investigation and corrective action from the supplier.
- Work with the supplier to identify and fix the root cause.
- Request compensation or replacement from the supplier if possible
body.preventiveAction:
- Tighter supplier QC: Increase checks on supplier products upon receipt.
- Audit supplier processes: Review supplier's hygiene and QC practices.
- Alternative suppliers?: Consider options if issue persists with current supplier.

body.detailsOfNC: The driver use this trolley to move the carcass, and the this trolley for garbage and he through the garbage on the floor, as the received a complaint from the manager.
body.correctiveAction:
- Designate specific trolleys for specific purposes (e.g., one for carcasses, another for garbage).
- Ensure the driver understands the importance of segregation.
body.preventiveAction:
- Stop using the trolley for both purposes ASAP.
- Clean and sanitize the trolley thoroughly.
- Address the driver's actions with him directly.

body.detailsOfNC: Customer made a complaint, as he ordered Frozen mined and on quotation was mentioned frozen minced, but the customer received Frozen Lamb Fat, and the invoice mentioned Frozen Fat, while speaking with helper and driver, they didn't check while loading the items inside his vehicle.
body.correctiveAction:
- Issue a corrected invoice for the actual order (Frozen Minced Meat).
- Review and rectify the mistake with the customer.
body.preventiveAction:
- Double-check orders: Implement a strict checking process during loading/unloading.
- Staff training: Emphasize importance of verifying products against orders.
- Clear labeling: Ensure products are clearly labeled to avoid mix-ups.

body.detailsOfNC: The customer complained about the weight that there is different between the actual weight on the driver scale and the weight in the invoice
body.correctiveAction:
- Investigate and verify the accuracy of both scales (driver's scale and invoicing system).
- Check for any inconsistencies in weighing procedures.
- Adjust invoice weight to match actual weight if error found.
- Communicate with customer and provide corrected invoice if needed.
- Review and improve internal processes to prevent future discrepancies.
body.preventiveAction:
- Calibrate scales regularly: Schedule periodic calibration and maintenance of all weighing scales.
- Standardize weighing procedures: Ensure consistent weighing methods across all locations.
- Double-check weights: Implement a system for verifying weights before invoicing.
- Train staff: Provide training on accurate weighing and data entry practices.
- Internal audits: Conduct regular checks to ensure compliance with procedures.
